data.title: Citizens Financial targets 3.05% to 3.10% NIM by year-end 2025 with strategic initiatives
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Citizens Financial Group (CFG) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Bruce Van Saun highlighted a 3 basis point expansion in net interest margin (NIM) to 2.90% and core loan growth of 1% for the quarter. The company executed a sale of $1.9 billion in non-core student loans to enhance NIM, EPS, and ROTCE, with $200 million already sold and the remainder to settle over the year.
  • The Private Bank reported strong growth, with deposits reaching $8.7 billion and assets under management (AUM) at $5.2 billion, including the addition of new teams in Florida, Southern California, and New Jersey.
  • CFO John Woods reported EPS of $0.77 and ROTCE of 9.6%. He noted NIM improvement was driven by non-core runoff and better deposit cost management. The company executed $200 million in share buybacks during the quarter, supported by a strong CET1 ratio of 10.64%.

Outlook

  • Management reaffirmed its full-year EPS guidance and expects net interest income to grow by 3% in Q2 2025, driven by a 5 basis point improvement in NIM. Non-interest income is projected to increase mid- to high-single digits, reflecting capital markets and wealth management growth.
  • The company continues to target a year-end NIM range of 3.05% to 3.10%, supported by ongoing benefits from non-core runoff and improved deposit costs.

Financial Results

  • Net interest income declined 1.5% quarter-over-quarter due to fewer days in the period and lower interest-earning assets, despite an improved NIM.
  • Non-interest income fell 3.5% sequentially, reflecting seasonal declines in capital markets and card fees. M&A and loan syndication activity in the capital markets were impacted by market uncertainty, but pipelines for M&A are at record highs.
  • Period-end deposits grew by approximately $3 billion, with the Private Bank contributing $1.7 billion of that increase. Interest-bearing deposit costs decreased by 18 basis points sequentially.

Q&A

  • John Pancari, Evercore ISI: Questioned loan demand trends and the impact of pre-tariff inventory builds. CFO John Woods noted increased line utilization in commercial banking and strong performance in residential and home equity lending, as well as consistent growth in the Private Bank.
  • Ken Usdin, Autonomous Research: Asked about capital markets fee guidance and offsets in a challenging environment. CEO Van Saun cited a diverse fee structure and highlighted strong M&A pipelines, while CFO Woods emphasized cost controls as a potential offset to slower M&A activity.
  • Erika Najarian, UBS: Asked about the medium-term reserve outlook. CFO Woods explained that the economic forecast includes a mild recession assumption with an unemployment rate of 5.1% for most portfolios, but higher for CRE-related reserves.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism about loan demand and capital markets recovery but raised concerns about macroeconomic uncertainty and its impact on growth.
  • Management maintained a confident tone during prepared remarks, emphasizing resilience and strategic progress, but displayed a more cautious approach during Q&A when discussing economic risks and deal pipelines.
  • Compared to Q4 2024, management's tone reflected increased confidence in NIM trajectory and Private Bank performance, while analysts remained consistent in their scrutiny of macro challenges.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • NIM improved by 3 basis points in Q1 2025 versus 10 basis points in Q4 2024.
  • Loan growth of 1% in Q1 2025 contrasts with the subdued activity in prior quarters.
  • Analysts' focus shifted from broader strategic initiatives to specific concerns about capital markets and economic uncertainties.
  • Management showed greater confidence in achieving year-end NIM targets and Private Bank contributions compared to the previous quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified economic uncertainty, slower capital markets recovery, and potential credit quality concerns as key risks.
  • Analysts questioned the impact of delayed M&A activity and higher unemployment assumptions on financial projections.
  • The company highlighted its strategic flexibility in managing costs and optimizing its balance sheet to mitigate potential headwinds.

Final Takeaway

Citizens Financial Group delivered a solid Q1 2025 performance with notable NIM improvement and Private Bank growth. Management reaffirmed its year-end NIM target of 3.05% to 3.10% and highlighted strategic initiatives to navigate macroeconomic challenges. Analysts remained cautiously optimistic, with attention on capital markets recovery and economic risks.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Comstock surges after entering solar recycling partnership with RWE
data.description:
Comstock (NYSE:LODE) +14.9% in Wednesday's trading after saying it signed a master services agreement to provide renewable energy company RWE (OTCPK:RWEOY) with recycling, decommissioning, and logistics services for its U.S. solar installations.

Under the deal terms, Comstock Metals (NYSE:LODE) will serve as RWE's (OTCPK:RWEOY) preferred partner for projects including the recycling of solar panels and related equipment, logistics management, eco-friendly disposal practices, and the safe transportation of materials.

Comstock (LODE) said the agreement builds on the existing partnership between the two companies after working together on multiple projects in Nevada and California, already coordinating the decommissioning, transportation, and recycling of more than 4M lbs of end-of-life solar materials for RWE (OTCPK:RWEOY), with more anticipated as demand for responsible recycling grows.

Comstock (LODE) "complements RWE's efforts as a trusted provider in the renewable energy market, ensuring environmentally conscious recycling of the solar panels and their components," Comstock Metals President Fortunato Villamagna said.

data.title: S&P, Nasdaq fall on tech rout amid more trade war concerns, Dow seesaws
data.description:
Major averages pared some of their losses on Wednesday, amid worries about a U.S.-China trade war rose on news that Nvidia will need a special license to ship graphic processing units to China.

The S&P 500 (SP500) was last -0.6%, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was -1.4%. The Dow (DJI) had turned marginally higher.

The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was down 2 basis points to 4.32%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) was lower by 5 basis points to 3.80%.

The core retail sales beat consensus in March, while the headline number came in line

Wall Street on Tuesday ended slightly lower in a choppy trading session, as a boost from positive bank earnings petered out amid more caution over developments surrounding tariffs.

"After a strong start to the week, the market mood turned more negative again yesterday, as tensions between the US and China showed signs of further escalation," Deutsche Bank's Henry Allen said.

"So after a brief period of greater stability in markets, that reminded investors about the ongoing risks of escalation, raising fears that the trade war could still get worse from here," Allen added.

Nvidia (NVDA) fell 6.5% as it received a notice from the U.S. government that it needs a special license to ship its H20 graphic processing units or related hardware to China, and such a license will be required indefinitely.

"Some politicians may believe trade is more important than it is. Trade does matter economically, but a 'made in China' product in a store does not mean all the price paid goes to China," UBS' Paul Donovan said.

President Donald Trump has also directed an investigation to determine if U.S. critical minerals imports "threaten to impair national security," which could lead to tariffs or restrictions on the sector.

Also on the economic side, industrial production dipped slightly more than expected in March to 0.3% M/M, weaker than the -0.2% consensus figure.

Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell is also expected to speak later in the day.

data.title: The risk of a recession is ‘significantly underpriced’ – GlobalData TS Lombard
data.description:
Equity markets rallied briefly after the Trump administration paused new tariffs, but GlobalData TS Lombard analysts warn this optimism ignores gathering storm clouds.

“The market isn’t accurately pricing the prospective hit to growth from Trump’s policies,” said Daniel von Ahlen, senior macro strategist at GlobalData TS Lombard, in a note.

He cited consensus GDP forecasts of 1.8% for 2024 remaining overly optimistic, given looming labor market strains and inflationary pressures. According to the note, these factors could significantly impact economic growth.

Markets are underestimating the cumulative drag from Washington’s policy shifts, von Ahlen said. DOGE austerity measures, aggressive immigration crackdowns, and China’s retaliatory tariffs threaten to shrink the labor force, squeeze corporate margins, and erode consumer spending power.

“The spending cuts required to extend TCJA tax reductions will further weigh on growth,” he added, warning these factors could tip the economy into recession.

Over the equity markets, U.S. stocks (SP500), (COMP:IND), (DJI) face heightened vulnerability despite recent declines, the investment bank analyst said. 

The note highlighted a disconnect between the S&P 500’s (SP500) 20x forward P/E ratio and rising recession risks, with consensus still projecting 8.9% earnings growth for 2025.

“A recession would collapse EPS estimates, leaving valuations at levels inconsistent with historical stress periods,” von Ahlen cautioned, recommending defensive sector longs (XLU), (XLP), (XLV) over cyclicals (XLF), (XLI), (XLY).

The firm upgraded U.S. bonds to a long position, favoring 15-year+ TIPS via the PIMCO 15+ Year U.S. TIPS Index Exchange-Traded Fund ETF (LTPZ).

“Real yield-driven treasury selloffs create an attractive entry point,” GlobalData TS Lombard analysts said, adding that President Trump’s manufacturing push risks anchoring higher goods inflation – a dynamic not yet reflected in breakeven rates.

According to the note, widening term premiums are emerging as markets struggle to price Federal Reserve responses to “fat-tail” outcomes of recession or runaway inflation.

In currencies, analysts initiated a long Japanese Yen/Mexican Peso (JPY:MXN) trade predicated on escalating U.S. downturn risks, analysts said.

“Mexico’s growth is tethered to the U.S. cycle, while yen undervaluation and falling energy prices support a reversal,” the note stated. Also, with Banxico (Bank of Mexico) expected to slash rates amid global disinflation, the peso faces headwinds absent in Japan’s improving terms of trade.

GlobalData TS Lombard reiterated that markets remain “dangerously complacent” about 2025 recession catalysts.

“The convergence of fiscal drag, policy uncertainty, and evaporating income growth leaves minimal margin for error,” von Ahlen said, urging investors to hedge via defensives, duration, and yen exposure ahead of potential growth shocks. These protective measures are increasingly important as economic indicators continue to deteriorate.

data.title: California attorney general opts out of joining Elon Musk lawsuit against OpenAI
data.description:
The California attorney general has opted out of joining Elon Musk's lawsuit against his former company, OpenAI.

In a publicly available letter, the attorney general's office said it did not see merit to Musk suing the Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) backed ChatGPT maker in the context of benefiting the public interest. The letter also suggested that Musk wants OpenAI's charitable arm for his own benefit. 

Last month, a federal judge in California rejected Musk's request to block OpenAI's transition from a nonprofit to a for-profit entity but accepted to expedite a trial to consider Musk’s claims against OpenAI and its CEO.

The lawsuit was recently amended to include Microsoft and several other parties, including LinkedIn co-founder Reid Hoffman and Dee Templeton, a current Microsoft executive who previously worked at OpenAI.

Earlier this year, a group led by Musk made an all-cash unsolicited $97.4B offer for control of OpenAI. The offer is good until May 10, 2025, three months after the letter of intent sent to OpenAI's board of directors was dated.

OpenAI Chief Executive Sam Altman, who co-founded OpenAI in 2015, along with Musk, said the company is not for sale.

The California AG has to approve OpenAI's transition to being a for-profit entity, since it is based in the state. Many groups and parties, including Meta Platforms (META), have tried to stop OpenAI's transition, Reuters reported.

OpenAI recently finished a new funding round that valued the company at $300B. However, lead investor SoftBank (OTCPK:SFTBY) required OpenAI to transition to an independent, for-profit company by year's end.

The case is set to go before a jury in spring 2026.

data.title: Retail sales benefited from consumers stocking up ahead of tariff-induced price hikes
data.description:
Blowout retail sales numbers for March were not enough to lift shares of Amazon (AMZN), Walmart (WMT), Target (TGT), and Costco (COST) as investors looked at the report as an indication that shoppers rushed out to stock up on household items on the expectation of higher prices down the road due to painful tariffs.

Retail sales rose 1.4% in March, after rising 0.2% in February, according to the U.S. Census Bureau. Excluding sales at dealers of autos and parts, sales only rose 0.5% in March, compared with the previous month.

"Consumers are expecting sharply higher prices the next year and are clearing the store shelves and picking up bargains while they can," warned FWDBonds Chief Economist Christopher Rupkey on the report. Northlight Asset Management Chief Investment Office Chris Zaccarelli also said consumers are front-loading purchases to give the retail sales tally an artificial bump for the month.

One of the positive readouts from the report was that the building material & garden category showed a 3.3% month-over-month and 2.6% year-over-year increase in March, which was higher than anticipated and could support home improvement retailers Depot (HD) and Lowe's (LOW) for the crucial spring selling season. Meanwhile, the furniture and home furnishing stores category showed a 7.7% increase, which could indicate consumers pushed up some significant household spending decisions. Notably, the health & personal care stores category was up 7.2% from a year ago. 

Once again, department stores performed weak, with a 0.3% month-over-month and 2.5% year-over-year decline as Macy's (M), Kohl's (KSS), Nordstrom (JWN), and Dillard's (DDS) continue to struggle to keep up with retail peers.

The SPDR S&P Retail ETF (NYSEARCA:XRT) was down 0.4% in late morning trading. EBay (EBAY) was one of the notable gainers in the retail sector, with a 2.1% swing higher. Restaurant stocks Papa John's International (PZZA) +1.8%, Dine Brands Global (DIN) +1.1%, and BJ's Restaurants (BJ) +1.0% were also outperformers after the restaurant sales category outpaced broad retail sales in the March report.

data.title: Trade war could trigger a year-long recession in Canada, Bank of Canada says
data.description:
With the unpredictability of U.S. trade policy, the Bank of Canada offered two outlook scenarios in its April Monetary Policy Report. "There is a range of scenarios for how US trade policy could unfold, which leads to a wide range of outcomes for Canadian inflation and economic growth," the central bank said on Wednesday.

As expected, the Bank of Canada kept its overnight rate at 2.75% while it monitors the economic situation and the impacts of potential U.S. tariffs.

In the more extreme scenario, a prolonged trade war with the U.S. would push Canada into a year-long recession, boosting inflation to over 3% and permanently reducing the standard of living in the country.  With a more limited tariff regime, Canada's gross domestic product would stall for one quarter and inflation drop below target for the rest of 2025 and in early 2026.

In the event of a broad trade war, U.S. tariffs would reduce Canada's potential output, and GDP would average about -1.2% for four quarters, then gradually recover through the rest of 2026 and 2027, the Bank of Canada estimates. Exports would fall sharply in mid-2026, and imports would also contract. Domestic demand would stall in Q2-Q3 2025. Canadian exporters would reduce production and lay off workers, spurring higher unemployment, a decline in real incomes, and a slowdown in household spending.

Under the milder trade conflict scenario, Canadian exports would drop sharply in Q2 2025, but then pick up in the second half of 2025 into 2027. GDP would stall in Q2 2025 then average 1.6% through the end of 2027.

In midmorning trading, The S&P/TSX Composite Index rose 0.4%, and the Canadian dollar (CAD:USD) gained 0.5% against the U.S. greenback. The iShares MSCI Canada ETF (NYSEARCA:EWC) rose 0.8%, and the Invesco CurrencyShares Canadian Dollar ETF (NYSEARCA:FXC) increased 0.6%.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Ecolab downgraded at Wells Fargo on weaker end markets, tariff pressure
data.description:
Ecolab (NYSE:ECL) on Wednesday was downgraded to Equal Weight from a previous investment rating of Overweight by analysts at Wells Fargo Securities. They said the provider of water, hygiene and infection-prevention products faces deteriorating conditions in its end markets and growing macroeconomic uncertainty, including potential challenges from tariffs.

Analyst Jason Haas said in an April 16 report that while Ecolab's (NYSE:ECL) earnings per share may remain on track, volume pressure and slower organic growth could weigh on the stock’s performance in the coming quarters.

Ecolab’s (ECL) organic constant currency growth is likely to fall short of prior guidance, prompting the downgrade, Haas said. The company is expected to revise its OCC outlook to around 3%, down from the previous projection of more than 4% for 2025.

However, earnings guidance is likely to remain unchanged, buoyed by cost-reduction initiatives and less severe foreign exchange effects than initially forecasted.

Ecolab’s (ECL) industrial segment appears especially vulnerable. Haas said declining industrial production and weaker PMI data are signs of a broader slowdown. Though the high-tech vertical may offer some long-term promise, it currently accounts for just 2% of revenue and is not positioned to offset softness elsewhere. In addition, food production volumes were muted in the first quarter, while paper manufacturers continue to struggle amid elevated natural gas prices.

Institutional, healthcare under pressure

The Institutional & Specialty segment (I&S) is also facing challenges. Haas pointed to a deceleration in restaurant traffic through the first quarter, supported by both Placer.ai data and commentary from McDonald’s, which reported a slowdown in February.

Meanwhile, Ecolab’s (ECL) healthcare business, now included in I&S and representing roughly $700 million in sales, is expected to drag on margins by 100 basis points because of a mix of business exits and pricing adjustments.

While stabilization is expected longer-term, the segment is not positioned to drive near-term upside.

Life sciences weakness, pest control resilience

Wells Fargo also is concerned about Ecolab’s Life Sciences business (approximately 4% of sales), which could see challenges later in the year from cuts in research funding at the National Institutes of Health. Although this effect may not fully materialize in the first quarter, Haas said investor sentiment toward the segment has already declined significantly.

On a brighter note, the Pest Control unit (7% of revenue) remains resilient, with solid demand reported despite colder temperatures compared with a year earlier. However, Haas said Rollins (ROL) is preferrable as a more focused play on the pest control market. Wells Fargo currently rates the company Overweight.

Valuation and outlook

Wells Fargo cut its price target for Ecolab (ECL to $240 from $265 a share, now based on 29 times projected 2026 earnings per share, down from 31 times previously.

The bank also lowered its OCC growth forecasts to 2.7% for 2025 and 3.4% for 2026 (from 4.8% and 4.9%) and adjusted its EPS estimates to $7.50 and $8.27, down from $7.55 and $8.47, respectively.

“Protecting EPS via cost cuts and FX may not be enough to support the stock,” Haas said, warning that tariff-related uncertainty could further weigh on industrial performance.

data.description:

data.title: Abbott spikes as management addresses tariff concerns
data.description:
Abbott Laboratories (NYSE:ABT) added ~5% to record its best intraday gain in nearly two years on Wednesday after the company reaffirmed its full-year outlook while estimating “a few hundred million dollars” of impact related to tariffs.

Earlier in the day, the North Chicago, Illinois-based healthcare giant reaffirmed its earnings outlook despite posting an earnings beat with its Q1 2025 results.

The company also announced a $500M investment in the U.S. to expand two of its existing manufacturing sites and boost its R&D efforts, a move widely seen as a response to the Trump administration’s newly introduced tariffs on MedTech products and potentially new tariffs on pharmaceuticals.  

“Prior to the tariffs, given the momentum that we were seeing in the base business, we've been considering raising our EPS guidance,” CEO Robert Ford said, adding that “reaffirming our guidance is already, I think, a pretty strong statement.”

“We estimate the tariff impact in '25 to be a few hundred million dollars,” he projected, noting that the company, which operates 90 manufacturing sites globally, rules out tariff-related headwinds in Q2 2025. “That's a half-year impact because I don't see any impact in Q2,” Ford added.
